skill.name: configuring-tmux
skill.description: Guides tmux setup with status bar frameworks, plugins, multiple bars, and custom widgets. It covers oh-my-tmux, Catppuccin, tmux-powerline, TPM plugins, shell widgets, caching, and reload troubleshooting.
skill.summary: Configure tmux status bars, plugins, widgets, and multi-bar layouts.
skill.author: ibrahimhka

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, and filesystem patterns, but review shows they are mostly tmux setup examples and framework installation guidance. The confirmed risk is medium because following the skill can clone third-party repositories, change files under the user's home directory, create a forced tmux symlink, and configure tmux status command substitutions. No malicious intent or prompt injection attempt was found.

medium_findings.title: Home Directory tmux Configuration Changes
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to clone frameworks, copy tmux files, create a forced symlink, install TPM plugins, and source tmux config files. This matches the skill purpose, but it can overwrite or alter a user's existing tmux configuration if applied without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are explicit and semantically tied to tmux setup. The risk is environmental modification, not evidence of malware.

medium_findings.title: External Repository and API Access
medium_findings.description: The skill references GitHub clone commands for tmux frameworks and plugins, plus a Yahoo Finance curl request for a widget. These endpoints are relevant to the stated purpose, but they still introduce third-party network trust and availability risks.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network commands are directly visible and serve documented tmux installation and widget examples. No credential exfiltration or suspicious destination was found.

medium_findings.title: tmux Status Command Substitution
medium_findings.description: The skill teaches tmux status entries that execute shell scripts through command substitution. This is a normal tmux feature, but any script path placed there will run repeatedly inside the user's tmux session.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command-substitution behavior is explicit in tmux configuration examples. It is legitimate but can become unsafe if users insert untrusted scripts.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Detections Are Not Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported many Ruby backtick execution patterns, but the reviewed locations are Markdown code fences or inline command examples. The examples still include shell commands, but the Ruby-specific detection is a false positive.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files are Markdown documentation, not Ruby source. The backticks delimit examples rather than executing code.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged weak cryptography at descriptive text locations, but no cryptographic algorithm or password hashing implementation appears in the reviewed files. No evidence found for real cryptographic behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines describe the skill and do not contain crypto APIs, hashes, or encryption instructions. This is a strong false positive.

low_findings.title: Troubleshooting Commands Are Benign
low_findings.description: The reported reconnaissance-like patterns are tmux troubleshooting references, such as checking tmux options and known status bar mistakes. They do not enumerate host secrets or collect system identity data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands inspect tmux configuration state for troubleshooting. I found no semantic evidence of host reconnaissance beyond normal tmux diagnostics.

dangerous_patterns.title: Forced Symlink to tmux Config
dangerous_patterns.description: The oh-my-tmux setup uses a forced symlink for the user's tmux config. This can replace an existing tmux config link or file if used without a backup.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The symlink command is explicit and uses force behavior. The risk is configuration overwrite, not malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Repeated Shell Execution from Status Bar
dangerous_patterns.description: tmux status examples execute scripts from the user's config directory. Those scripts can run repeatedly when the tmux status bar refreshes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command-substitution syntax is visible in both files. This is normal tmux behavior but security-sensitive when script content is untrusted.

dangerous_patterns.title: Third-Party Network Fetch for Finance Widget
dangerous_patterns.description: The finance widget example fetches market data from Yahoo Finance with curl. It uses a timeout and a public API endpoint, but it still makes external network requests.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network request is explicit and relevant to the widget example. I found no evidence that it sends local secrets.

content.user_title: Configure tmux Status Bars and Widgets
content.value_statement: tmux status bar setup can be hard because frameworks override different settings. This skill gives structured guidance for frameworks, plugins, widgets, multi-bar layouts, and reload troubleshooting.
actual_capabilities: Helps choose between oh-my-tmux, Catppuccin, tmux-powerline, or a user-provided framework.
Provides TPM plugin setup guidance for resurrect, continuum, CPU, and network speed plugins.
Explains tmux 3.2+ multi-bar configuration using status-format arrays.
Shows widget patterns for weather, finance, clocks, news tickers, and one-line script output.
Documents common reload and override problems for tmux status bars.
limitations: It does not verify that third-party tmux repositories are trustworthy.
It does not automatically back up existing tmux configuration files.
It assumes the user has tmux and shell tooling available.
Some widget examples require network access or external API availability.

use_cases.title: Build a new tmux setup
use_cases.description: Create a framework-based tmux configuration with plugins, status content, and reload steps.
use_cases.target_user: Developers setting up a new terminal environment

use_cases.title: Add useful status widgets
use_cases.description: Plan single-line widgets for clocks, finance, weather, CPU, memory, network speed, or news.
use_cases.target_user: Terminal users who want richer status information

use_cases.title: Fix status bar overrides
use_cases.description: Diagnose why tmux status settings disappear after reloads or framework updates.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a tmux framework
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose a tmux status bar framework. Ask about my terminal, font, plugin needs, and how much customization I want.

prompt_templates.title: Set up a basic status bar
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through a safe tmux status bar setup with TPM plugins. Include backup steps before changing my config.

prompt_templates.title: Add widgets to tmux
prompt_templates.prompt: Design tmux status widgets for clocks, CPU, memory, and finance. Keep scripts one line and cache network results.

prompt_templates.title: Debug a multi-bar layout
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my tmux multi-bar plan and explain why my changes may be overridden. Focus on status-format arrays and framework-specific settings.

output_examples.input: I want a second tmux bar with clocks and market data.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step plan that checks tmux version, sets the bar count, places widgets on an extra status row, and adds caching for network data.

output_examples.input: My oh-my-tmux status changes disappear after reload.
output_examples.output: A diagnosis explaining that the framework owns the main bar, with guidance to use theme variables or extra status-format rows.
A checklist covering repository installation, font requirements, TPM setup, plugin choices, and reload verification.

best_practices: Back up existing tmux files before applying framework setup commands.
Review every repository and script before adding it to tmux startup or status refresh.
Cache network widget output and keep each status script to one line.
anti_patterns: Do not run forced symlink commands without checking existing tmux files.
Do not put untrusted scripts inside tmux status command substitution.
Do not use invalid status options when status-format arrays are required.

faq.question: Does this skill install tmux itself?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on tmux configuration, frameworks, plugins, status bars, and widgets.

faq.question: Which tmux version is needed for multiple bars?
faq.answer: The skill states that tmux 3.2 or later supports multiple status bars.

faq.question: Can it work with Catppuccin?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Catppuccin guidance for TPM-based setup and status modules.

faq.question: Can it help with oh-my-tmux overrides?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains that oh-my-tmux owns the main bar and uses theme variables for customization.

faq.question: Are network widgets safe by default?
faq.answer: They require review. The skill recommends caching, but users should inspect any network script before use.

faq.question: Does it support custom frameworks?
faq.answer: Yes. It tells the assistant to ask for the repository URL, install method, customization files, and status handling.
